target.title: Doctor: structure config audit and cron repairs
target.body:
## Goal

Move config audit scrub and legacy cron repairs into the structured Doctor repair contract.

This wave covers repairs where diffs matter most: config cleanup, audit scrub behavior, and legacy cron migration. Existing repair policy stays intact, while dry-run and repair output now expose concrete `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs` where the repair can describe file edits.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Config audit and cron repairs used to be command-local repair flows with presentation mixed into the mutation path. This PR keeps the same repair decisions while making the output isomorphic:

- `detect()` reports config audit and cron findings without mutation
- `repair()` receives the selected findings and performs only those repairs
- dry-run reports what would be scrubbed, migrated, or rewritten
- diffs are emitted where file edits can be represented usefully
- repair effects and warnings give preview/reporting surfaces stable machine signal

## Converted Here

- config audit scrub repairs
- legacy cron repairs
- cron/config repair effects
- diffs where available for file-backed repair output
- focused tests around config/cron repair behavior and shared repair-flow behavior

## Review Surface

This is the config/cron-policy PR to challenge:

- whether config audit scrub findings are grouped at the right level for repair and preview
- whether cron migration effects should name legacy entries, target schedules, or files differently
- whether the emitted diffs are the right level of detail for `doctor --dry-run --diff`
- whether warnings represent real operator decisions rather than presentation text

## Stack Position

Parent: #84366. This PR is the config audit and cron wave in the Doctor repair stack.

The follow-on PR exposes the top-level dry-run/diff preview surface once enough repair buckets have structured output.

## Verification

- Restacked on #84366 as a single commit.
- Code search confirmed this branch does not reintroduce `HealthPresentationNote`, `repairNote`, `detectionNotes`, `repairNotes`, or structured note result surfaces.
- Changed test coverage includes config audit, cron, gateway, session, plugin doctor, health contribution, and repair-flow tests for this slice.
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Config audit and legacy cron repairs now use structured findings, scoped repair, dry-run output, repair effects, and diffs where available while preserving existing repair policy.

Real environment tested: Windows checkout on the refreshed stack.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: no focused command completed locally for this PR body refresh; rely on PR CI for broad proof.

Evidence after fix: The branch updates config audit and cron repair implementation and related tests so file-backed repairs can report structured changes/effects/warnings/diffs and keep presentation notes out of result payloads.

Observed result after fix: Config audit and cron repair behavior remains available through Doctor, while dry-run and later preview surfaces can inspect planned file edits before mutation.

What was not tested: Live cron migration scenarios and platform-wide Doctor CI were not rerun locally for this body refresh.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds Anthropic-compatible Bearer auth handling for `authHeader: true` providers at runtime and during custom endpoint verification, plus focused tests.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level. Current main still builds `x-api-key` in the Anthropic transport and custom verification helper, while the PR's save path drops the inferred Bearer mode before persisted config.
commands.review_followup: Needs contributor real behavior proof and a maintainer decision on refreshing or replacing the conflicting stale branch against the current transport architecture.

target.title: Fix anthropic auth header
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: custom providers using `api: "anthropic-messages"` did not honor `models.providers.*.authHeader` in the runtime request path, and Anthropic custom-endpoint verification also defaulted to Anthropic-style auth headers.
- Why it matters: Anthropic-compatible gateways/proxies that require `Authorization: Bearer <token>` could fail with authentication errors even when users explicitly configured `authHeader: true`.
- What changed: added a runtime wrapper that applies `Authorization: Bearer <apiKey>` for `anthropic-messages` providers when `authHeader === true`, clears `x-api-key` in that path, and made custom Anthropic endpoint verification respect the same setting.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default native Anthropic auth behavior did not change; custom Anthropic-compatible providers without `authHeader: true` are unchanged; no non-Anthropic adapters were modified.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Custom providers configured with `api: "anthropic-messages"` and `authHeader: true` now send `Authorization: Bearer <apiKey>` at runtime instead of relying on `x-api-key`.
- In the same configuration, custom Anthropic endpoint verification during onboarding now uses `Authorization: Bearer <apiKey>` instead of `x-api-key`.
- Default behavior for native Anthropic providers and custom Anthropic-compatible providers without `authHeader: true` is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This PR changes how existing API credentials are serialized into outbound HTTP headers for a narrow, explicitly opted-in case (`api: "anthropic-messages"` + `authHeader: true`).
  - Risk: sending both `Authorization` and `x-api-key` could create ambiguous auth behavior on some proxies.
  - Mitigation: in the opted-in path, the runtime explicitly injects `Authorization: Bearer ...` and clears `x-api-key` to avoid conflicting auth styles. The default path is unchanged.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 Pro 10.0.22631
- Runtime/container: local Node/pnpm dev environment
- Model/provider: custom provider with `api: "anthropic-messages"`
- Integration/channel (if any): None
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "custom-provider": {
        "baseUrl": "https://example.com",
        "api": "anthropic-messages",
        "authHeader": true,
        "models": [
          {
            "id": "claude-opus-4-6",
            "name": "Claude Opus 4.6",
            "reasoning": false,
            "input": ["text"],
            "cost": {
              "input": 0,
              "output": 0,
              "cacheRead": 0,
              "cacheWrite": 0
            },
            "contextWindow": 170000,
            "maxTokens": 64000
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure a custom provider with `api: "anthropic-messages"` and `authHeader: true`.
2. Trigger a runtime model call, or run custom endpoint verification during onboarding.
3. Observe the outbound auth header behavior.

### Expected

- Runtime and verification requests use `Authorization: Bearer <apiKey>`.
- `x-api-key` is not used in the opted-in path.

### Actual

- Before this patch, the Anthropic-compatible path did not honor `authHeader: true`.
- Runtime/verification behavior could still follow Anthropic-style auth semantics and fail against Bearer-only proxies/gateways.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran `pnpm test -- src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts`
  - Ran `pnpm test -- src/commands/onboard-custom.test.ts`
  - Confirmed the runtime wrapper injects `Authorization: Bearer ...` and clears `x-api-key` when `authHeader: true` is configured for a custom `anthropic-messages` provider.
  - Confirmed Anthropic custom-endpoint verification still defaults to `x-api-key` unless the existing provider config explicitly enables `authHeader: true`.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `authHeader: true` path for custom Anthropic-compatible providers
  - default/no-`authHeader` path remains unchanged
  - non-Anthropic adapters not touched by this patch
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Did not manually verify against a live third-party Anthropic-compatible gateway in this PR.
  - Did not run the full repository test suite; only focused suites for the touched paths were run.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Revert this commit, or stop setting `authHeader: true` for the affected custom Anthropic-compatible provider.
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/anthropic-stream-wrappers.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.ts`
  - `src/commands/onboard-custom.ts`
  - remove the related tests if reverting the behavior entirely
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - Native Anthropic requests unexpectedly switching to `Authorization`
  - Custom Anthropic-compatible providers receiving both `Authorization` and `x-api-key`
  - Onboarding verification using Bearer auth when `authHeader` is not enabled

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a custom provider with `api: "anthropic-messages"` and `authHeader: true` might rely on the previous `x-api-key` behavior despite the config name implying Authorization-header auth.
  - Mitigation: the behavior change is gated strictly behind explicit opt-in (`authHeader: true`), and the default path remains unchanged.
- Risk: runtime and onboarding verification could diverge again in future changes.
  - Mitigation: added focused tests for both runtime wrapper behavior and onboarding verification behavior.
- Risk: clearing `x-api-key` could expose assumptions in downstream code/tests.
  - Mitigation: covered the opted-in path explicitly in tests and preserved all other existing headers (such as `anthropic-version`).

target.title: Audit config backups for plaintext secrets
target.body:
## Summary

/claim #11829

This closes a concrete remaining gap from the API-key protection roadmap: stale adjacent `openclaw.json` backups and managed snapshots can still contain plaintext credentials after the active config has moved to SecretRefs. `openclaw secrets audit` now scans those adjacent config artifacts and reports plaintext credential targets found there.

## Changes

- Add `listConfigBackupPaths()` coverage for rotation backups plus managed adjacent snapshots: `openclaw.json.bak`, `openclaw.json.bak.*`, `openclaw.json.*.bak`, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.*`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.*`.
- Parse backup/snapshot files with config-compatible JSON5 semantics instead of strict `JSON.parse`.
- Report unreadable, oversized, or unparsable backup snapshots as audit findings instead of silently treating them as clean.
- Report plaintext findings with the backup/snapshot file path and JSON path so users can clean stale copies.
- Add regression coverage for `.bak`, managed adjacent snapshots, and JSON5-authored backups.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw secrets audit --json` reports plaintext provider secrets from adjacent config backup/snapshot files while the active config uses an env SecretRef.

Real environment tested: local source checkout on Node.js 22.22.0 with a temporary OpenClaw state directory at `$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw`. The active `openclaw.json` used `apiKey: { source: "env", provider: "default", id: "OPENAI_API_KEY" }`. Adjacent stale snapshots contained redacted plaintext keys in `models.providers.openai.apiKey`: `openclaw.json.bak` with JSON5 comments/trailing commas, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
rm -rf tmp/cli-proof
mkdir -p tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw
node -e 'write active SecretRef config plus adjacent stale snapshots'
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-redacted-live-env-key" \
pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json
```

Evidence after fix: redacted terminal output from the real CLI command above:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "status": "findings",
  "resolution": {
    "refsChecked": 1,
    "skippedExecRefs": 0,
    "resolvabilityComplete": true
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z"
  ],
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 4,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 0,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "findings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real CLI output shows `status: findings`, scans the active config plus all four adjacent backup/snapshot files, reports `plaintextCount: 4`, and emits one `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` finding for each stale adjacent config artifact. The active config SecretRef is resolvable (`refsChecked: 1`, `unresolvedRefCount: 0`).

What was not tested: I did not run a packaged npm install smoke for `openclaw secrets audit`; this proof uses the source-checkout CLI path via `pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json`. I also did not test Windows filesystem snapshot names manually beyond the repository test suite.

## Validation

```text
pnpm exec vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts -t "adjacent config"

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       3 passed | 23 skipped (26)
```

```text
pnpm exec vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts -t "JSON5 semantics"

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       1 passed | 25 skipped (26)
```

```text
pnpm exec vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       26 passed (26)
```

`git diff --check` passes.

## Bounty

TaskBounty task: https://www.task-bounty.com/tasks/fix-security-roadmap-protecting-api-keys-from-agen-uej5sq

target.title: Doctor: structure sandbox repair checks
target.body:
## Goal

Move sandbox registry, image, and scope repairs into the structured Doctor repair contract.

This wave converts sandbox repair behavior from legacy prompt-oriented flows into finding-scoped detection and repair. The user-visible sandbox repair behavior stays the same, while the repair path now reports concrete `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and dry-run output that later preview surfaces can consume.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Sandbox doctor checks historically used local prompter/reporting behavior around registry, image, and scope cleanup. This PR keeps that interactive behavior but makes the contract isomorphic:

- `detect()` reports sandbox findings without mutation
- `repair()` receives the selected sandbox findings and performs the existing repair behavior
- dry-run describes the sandbox actions instead of performing them
- repair effects make sandbox mutations visible to later preview/reporting layers
- legacy `DoctorPrompter.note(...)` style presentation is bridged into `ctx.doctor.note(...)`

## Converted Here

- sandbox registry checks
- sandbox image checks
- sandbox scope checks
- sandbox repair effects and dry-run behavior
- focused sandbox and shared repair-flow test coverage around the converted paths

## Review Surface

This is the sandbox-policy PR to challenge:

- whether sandbox image, registry, and scope findings are split at the right granularity
- whether dry-run effects should distinguish cleanup, pull/build, and config actions differently
- whether the repair methods should expose richer diffs for sandbox config edits
- whether sandbox presentation notes are correctly limited to `ctx.doctor.note(...)`

## Stack Position

Parent: #84290. This PR is the sandbox wave in the Doctor repair stack.

Follow-on PRs continue the same contract through gateway services, session state, config audit/cron, and the top-level dry-run preview surface.

## Verification

- Restacked on #84290 as a single commit.
- Code search confirmed this branch does not reintroduce `HealthPresentationNote`, `repairNote`, `detectionNotes`, `repairNotes`, or structured note result surfaces.
- `git diff --check` passed during conflict resolution.
- Changed test coverage includes sandbox, plugin doctor, health contribution, and repair-flow tests for this slice.
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: Sandbox doctor repairs now follow structured detection, finding-scoped repair, dry-run output, and repair effects while preserving existing sandbox repair policy.

Real environment tested: Windows checkout on the refreshed stack.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check` during stack refresh; no focused Vitest command completed locally for this PR body refresh.

Evidence after fix: The branch updates sandbox doctor implementation and tests so sandbox repairs report structured effects/changes/warnings and keep presentation notes out of result payloads.

Observed result after fix: Sandbox repair behavior remains available through Doctor, while dry-run and later preview surfaces can reason over the same structured repair output.

What was not tested: Full sandbox runtime scenarios and platform-wide Doctor CI were not rerun locally for this body refresh.

target.title: Doctor: convert interactive maintenance checks
target.body:
## Goal

Start the repair-side migration for structured `openclaw doctor` checks with a small set of interactive maintenance repairs.

The structured health-check contract already gives doctor a read-only detection shape:

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
repair(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
```

This PR is where the repair half starts doing real work. It applies that shape to repairs that prompt, mutate state, report effects, support dry-run previews, and need post-repair validation. The walkthroughs here tighten the contract future repair conversions should follow: `detect()` remains the read-only source of truth; `repair()` receives the exact findings it may fix; repair output is machine-useful through `changes`, `warnings`, `diffs`, and `effects`.

The broader repair wave uses that same output shape so doctor can eventually show dry-run plans, diffs, and effects consistently for repairable findings. This base PR does not turn that richer reporting into the default user surface by itself; the follow-on chain at the end converts the remaining repair buckets first so the feature can be enabled once the migrated majority has the same contract.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; no `detect().notes`, no `repair().notes`, no runner note arrays.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Legacy interactive doctor code decided what to find, what to ask, what to mutate, and what to print inside local flows. This PR starts moving that behavior into an isomorphic health-check shape: the same `detect()` result can power lint, repair selection, dry-run reporting, and post-repair validation. The repair path remains interactive where it already was interactive, but the output is now structured enough for later preview and automation surfaces.

## Converted Here

This PR migrates the first interactive maintenance checks into structured doctor repairs:

- MCP invalid entry removal
- MCP stale config repair
- MCP safe config cleanup
- MCP API migration
- legacy project migration
- ambiguous agent path detection
- invalid agent metadata detection
- orphan agent cleanup

These are useful base cases because they cover prompts, filesystem mutation, dry-run previews, and follow-up validation without reaching into plugin-specific or provider-specific policy.

## Review Surface

This is the contract slice to challenge before the rest of the stack builds on it:

- whether `repair(ctx, findings)` is the right repair boundary for converted doctor checks
- whether detect-after validation should run after every successful non-dry-run repair
- whether `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs` are the right machine-readable repair vocabulary
- whether notes should stay purely presentation-only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`

## Follow-On Stack

This is the base PR for the current doctor repair stack:

| PR | Area |
| --- | --- |
| #84290 | plugin registry repairs |
| #84326 | sandbox repairs |
| #84340 | gateway service repairs |
| #84366 | session state repairs |
| #84450 | cron/config audit repairs |
| #84472 | dry-run preview reports |

Those PRs should build on this contract instead of reintroducing per-flow repair notes, ad hoc lint output, or local presentation models.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts`
- Existing CI should provide broader validation for the full stack after the refreshed single-commit PRs are replayed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: This PR starts the structured Doctor repair migration by applying finding-scoped repair, machine-readable repair results, presentation-only notes through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`, and detect-after validation to the first interactive maintenance repairs.

Real environment tested: Windows checkout at commit `5fdf0ff7329130f32c22f8e1cf47688a9071bf1f`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: `src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts` passed 7 focused tests covering structured repair execution, repair filtering, dry-run behavior, and detect-after repair validation.

Observed result after fix: The converted repair paths expose findings and repair outputs only. Human notes flow through the existing suppressible note system when a doctor runner provides `ctx.doctor.note(...)`. Lint remains findings-only.

What was not tested: Full doctor-stack CI and platform-wide doctor scenarios were not rerun locally for this body refresh; those should run through normal PR CI across the stack.

target.title: memory-lancedb: add configurable timeout/retry for embedding calls
target.body:
Replaces #56517 (closed, could not reopen).

- Add embedding.timeoutMs (default 10s) and embedding.maxRetries (default 1) to memory-lancedb plugin config
- Prevents cascading Connection error failures during API rate-limit storms
- Also bumps LaunchAgent ThrottleInterval from 1s to 10s

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `memory-lancedb`'s OpenAI-SDK embedding client had no operator-tunable timeout/retry budget. A hung or 5xx-storming embedding backend blocks on the SDK default (600000ms) and stalls every agent turn that triggers auto-recall. This PR lets operators bound it via `embedding.timeoutMs` / `embedding.maxRetries`. Per ClawSweeper review, the bounds now apply ONLY when explicitly configured; unset installs keep the SDK defaults (no upgrade behavior change).
- **Real environment tested**: local `openclaw` checkout (upgrade onto current upstream/main) with the bundled `openai` SDK the plugin loads at runtime, exercised against a blackhole TCP endpoint that accepts the connection but never responds (simulating a hung embedding backend).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # blackhole server + the same OpenAI client the plugin constructs, with the
  # configured bound applied:
  node ./embed-timeout-proof.mjs   # full script in PR discussion
  # core: new OpenAI({ baseURL: <blackhole>, timeout: 2000, maxRetries: 1 })
  #         .embeddings.create({ model, input })
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: captured live from the blackhole run via `node` against the bundled `openai` SDK in `~/.openclaw`/local checkout:
  ```
  blackhole embedding endpoint listening at http://127.0.0.1:60765/v1 (never responds)
  configured timeoutMs=2000 maxRetries=1: failed after 4520ms — APIConnectionTimeoutError: Request timed out.
  ```
  The configured bound aborts at 2000ms, retries once, and surfaces `APIConnectionTimeoutError` after ~4.5s total — instead of hanging on the SDK default 600000ms. The deployed wiring in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` now spreads `timeout`/`maxRetries` into the `new OpenAI({...})` constructor only when set (`...(timeoutMs !== undefined ? { timeout: timeoutMs } : {})`), so unconfigured installs retain SDK defaults.
- **Observed result after fix**: With `embedding.timeoutMs` set, a hung endpoint fails fast at the configured bound. With it unset, the OpenAI SDK default (600000ms / 2 retries) is preserved unchanged — no regression for installs with slow-but-working endpoints (the upgrade risk ClawSweeper flagged). Config-layer clamping still rejects out-of-range values (`timeoutMs` to [1000, 60000], `maxRetries` to [0, 5]).
- **What was not tested**: A real upstream OpenAI 5xx wave (used a local blackhole to deterministically reproduce the hang). Behavior on a partially-responsive endpoint that sends headers then stalls mid-body (the SDK's bodyTimeout governs that path, unchanged by this PR).

target.title: Warn on plaintext secret config in doctor
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a `doctor` security warning when `openclaw.json` contains plaintext secret-bearing fields such as `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` and sensitive provider headers like `Authorization`.
- Skip the warning for SecretRef-backed values so migrated configs stay quiet.
- Point users to `openclaw secrets configure` / `openclaw secrets apply` and `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

## Why

Issue #11829 tracks API key exposure to agents. The current secrets tooling can already audit and migrate plaintext values, but a normal `openclaw doctor` run can still look healthy while `openclaw.json` stores agent-readable API keys/tokens. This change makes that risk visible in the security warning surface users already run.

This does not claim to replace the broader secret-isolation architecture. It closes a practical visibility gap and helps users migrate away from plaintext config secrets before agents or workspace tools read them directly.

## Test

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts
```

Result: 1 test file passed, 30 tests passed.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` now surfaces plaintext secret-bearing model provider config in `openclaw.json`, including `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout after this patch, running `node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive` against a real temporary config file at `/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json`. The config contained redacted plaintext values in `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_HOME=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/home \
node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CLI command above included this live output excerpt:

```text
◇  Security

  - WARNING: openclaw.json contains plaintext secret-bearing config
    fields.
    Paths: models.providers.openai.apiKey,
    models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization
    Agents or workspace tools that can read config files may see these
    API keys/tokens.
    Migrate them to SecretRefs with openclaw secrets configure or
    openclaw secrets apply, then verify with openclaw secrets audit
    --check.
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live doctor run printed a Security warning for both plaintext config paths: `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`, and it pointed the user to SecretRefs plus `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

Refs #11829

target.title: Audit config backups for plaintext secrets
target.body:
## Summary

/claim #11829

This closes a concrete remaining gap from the API-key protection roadmap: stale adjacent `openclaw.json` backups and managed snapshots can still contain plaintext credentials after the active config has moved to SecretRefs. `openclaw secrets audit` now scans those adjacent config artifacts and reports plaintext credential targets found there.

## Changes

- Add `listConfigBackupPaths()` coverage for rotation backups plus managed adjacent snapshots: `openclaw.json.bak`, `openclaw.json.bak.*`, `openclaw.json.*.bak`, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.*`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.*`.
- Parse backup/snapshot files with config-compatible JSON5 semantics instead of strict `JSON.parse`.
- Report unreadable, oversized, or unparsable backup snapshots as audit findings instead of silently treating them as clean.
- Report plaintext findings with the backup/snapshot file path and JSON path so users can clean stale copies.
- Add regression coverage for `.bak`, managed adjacent snapshots, and JSON5-authored backups.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw secrets audit --json` reports plaintext provider secrets from adjacent config backup/snapshot files while the active config uses an env SecretRef.

Real environment tested: local source checkout on Node.js 22.22.0 with a temporary OpenClaw state directory at `$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw`. The active `openclaw.json` used `apiKey: { source: "env", provider: "default", id: "OPENAI_API_KEY" }`. Adjacent stale snapshots contained redacted plaintext keys in `models.providers.openai.apiKey`: `openclaw.json.bak` with JSON5 comments/trailing commas, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
rm -rf tmp/cli-proof
mkdir -p tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw
node -e 'write active SecretRef config plus adjacent stale snapshots'
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-redacted-live-env-key" \
pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json
```

Evidence after fix: redacted terminal output from the real CLI command above:

```json

    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z"
  ],
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 4,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 0,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "findings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"

      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",

      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real CLI output shows `status: findings`, scans the active config plus all four adjacent backup/snapshot files, reports `plaintextCount: 4`, and emits one `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` finding for each stale adjacent config artifact. The active config SecretRef is resolvable (`refsChecked: 1`, `unresolvedRefCount: 0`).

What was not tested: I did not run a packaged npm install smoke for `openclaw secrets audit`; this proof uses the source-checkout CLI path via `pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json`. I also did not test Windows filesystem snapshot names manually beyond the repository test suite.

## Validation

target.title: feat(core): implement session-aware heartbeat and system event normalization
target.body:
# Description

### Overview
This PR introduces a more robust synchronization and routing mechanism for background processes and system events. It ensures that AI agents operating in isolated sessions receive their execution results and heartbeats correctly without cross-session data leakage.

### Key Changes
- **Heartbeat Normalization**: Updated `infra/heartbeat-runner.ts` to use normalized session keys, ensuring the "wake-up" signal reaches the correct agent context.
- **Secure Event Routing**: Integrated `normalizeEventRoutingKey` within `services/bash-tools.exec.ts` to properly scope notifications and system events.
- **Enhanced System Events**: Refined the ephemeral queuing logic in `infra/system-events.ts` to strictly enforce `sessionKey` requirements.
- **Multi-Agent Support**: Standardized agent identification using `scopeKey` (Agent ID) to prevent event mix-ups.

### How to Test
1. Run a background bash process using a specific `sessionKey`.
2. Monitor the `infra/system-events` queue to ensure the event is correctly scoped.
3. Verify that the heartbeat only triggers for the relevant session upon process completion.

### Related Issues
- Fixes #71581 

### Affected Files
- `infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`
- `services/bash-tools.exec.ts`
- `security/dm-policy-shared.js`
- `infra/system-events.ts`

target.title: fix(status): honor selected usage auth profile
target.body:
- fixes #58498

## Summary

- `/status` already used `sessionEntry.authProfileOverride` for the auth label.
- Usage/quota resolution still selected credentials at the provider level.
- With multiple OAuth profiles under one provider, the auth label and the usage line could point at different profiles.
- This change passes the session-selected profile into provider usage resolution, prefers it when it belongs to the requested provider, and otherwise falls back to the existing provider-scoped order.

## Root cause

The status surface had two different auth-selection paths:

- the auth label respected the session override
- the usage loader did not

That split made the status card internally inconsistent for multi-profile OAuth setups.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  `/status` could show one OAuth profile as active while the usage/quota line reflected a different OAuth profile under the same provider.

- Real environment tested:
  Local OpenClaw development setup with multiple OAuth profiles configured for the same provider and a session-level `authProfileOverride` selecting a non-default profile.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Configure two OAuth profiles under the same provider.
  2. Select the secondary profile for the session with `authProfileOverride`.
  3. Run `/status`.
  4. Compare the auth label and the usage/quota line.

- Evidence after fix:
  Copied live output from a local `/status` run after the patch:

```text
/status
Model: openai/gpt-5.5
Auth: oauth (openai-codex:secondary)
Usage: 5h ... left · Week ... left
```

  In this after-fix output, the auth label and the usage-backed status line both resolve through the same selected OAuth profile.

- Observed result after fix:
  After the patch, `/status` used the same session-selected OAuth profile for both the auth label and the usage/quota line. A stale or cross-provider preferred profile was ignored and fell back to the existing provider-scoped order.

- What was not tested:
  I did not run a full provider-by-provider matrix or remote deployment verification. I focused on the affected multi-profile OAuth status flow.

## Regression coverage

- `src/infra/provider-usage.auth.normalizes-keys.test.ts`
- `src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts`
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`

target.title: feat(tts): add skipEmojiSymbols option to prevent TTS from reading emoji/symbols
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the opt-in `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` TTS preprocessing setting.
- Applies emoji/symbol stripping before file, stream, and telephony synthesis requests.
- Returns a clear no-op failure when stripping leaves no speakable text, instead of sending an empty provider request.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged when the option is disabled or unset.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Refs #77969
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

TTS can turn emoji and decorative symbols into noisy spoken names. Users who prefer cleaner voice output need a config-level way to strip those characters before synthesis without changing default behavior for everyone else.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: optional stripping of emoji/symbol characters before TTS synthesis across file, stream, and telephony paths; empty-after-strip input is blocked before provider calls.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, branch `tts-skip-v3`, commit `06545b841d08`, using the bundled real Microsoft speech provider path (`extensions/microsoft` -> `node-edge-tts` -> `speech.platform.bing.com`). No API keys or private account secrets were used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node --import tsx - <<'TS' ... register microsoft plugin, call textToSpeech twice with skipEmojiSymbols=false/true, then call an emoji-only enabled case ... TS`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
  - `pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod`
- After-fix evidence:
  ```text
  $ node --import tsx - <<'TS'
  # Registers the bundled Microsoft Speech plugin and performs real synthesis
  # through node-edge-tts / speech.platform.bing.com after this patch.
  TS
  {
    "branch": "tts-skip-v3",
    "commit": "06545b841d08",
    "provider": "microsoft",
    "realProviderPath": "extensions/microsoft -> node-edge-tts -> speech.platform.bing.com",
    "inputText": "Proof phrase: package ready. Check mark ✓ copyright © melting face 🫠 done.",
    "observed": [
      {
        "name": "disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": false,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/disabled-skipEmojiSymbols-false.mp3",
        "bytes": 39456,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      },
      {
        "name": "enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true",
        "skipEmojiSymbols": true,
        "success": true,
        "provider": "microsoft",
        "outputFormat": "audio-24khz-48kbitrate-mono-mp3",
        "target": "audio-file",
        "savedArtifact": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof/enabled-skipEmojiSymbols-true.mp3",
        "bytes": 36288,
        "headerHex": "fff364c4"
      }
    ],
    "emptyAfterStrip": {
      "success": false,
      "error": "TTS text is empty after removing emoji and symbol characters",
      "provider": null
    },
    "artifactsDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78172-real-proof"
  }

  $ ffprobe -v error -show_entries format=duration,size ...
  ffprobe was not installed in this container, so artifact validation used byte size plus MP3 frame header checks.

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       32 passed (32)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       14 passed (14)

  $ pnpm check:test-types && pnpm tsgo:prod
  exit_code=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: disabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio from the original text containing `✓`, `©`, and `🫠`; enabled mode generated real Microsoft speech audio after the shared speech-core filter removed those symbol/emoji characters; emoji/symbol-only enabled input failed before any provider was selected.
- What was not tested: live audio pronunciation across every vendor-specific provider. The real-provider proof uses the bundled Microsoft speech provider because it is credential-free and exercises the same speech-core preprocessing path that all providers share.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The original implementation filtered only some TTS entry points and did not guard the post-filter text before synthesis. The telephony path computed filtered text for setup but still passed the original text into provider synthesis, and emoji-only input could become an empty provider request.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/speech-core/src/tts.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/tts-tool.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenarios:
  - file synthesis strips complete emoji/symbol sequences when enabled.
  - stream synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - telephony synthesis receives stripped text when enabled.
  - telephony synthesis does not call providers when stripping leaves empty text.
  - tool-level config still passes original text into the shared TTS path, where effective config resolution owns filtering.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can enable `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols` to strip emoji/symbol characters before speech synthesis. Unset or `false` preserves existing behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
text -> effective TTS config -> optional emoji/symbol strip -> empty guard -> provider synthesis
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: live proof speech-provider plugin plus focused Vitest coverage
- Integration/channel: speech-core file, stream, telephony, and TTS tool paths
- Relevant config: `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols=true|false`

### Steps

1. Run the live proof script shown above.
2. Run focused speech-core tests.
3. Run focused TTS tool tests.
4. Run test and production type gates.

### Expected

Enabled stripping removes emoji/symbols before provider synthesis and blocks empty-after-strip requests. Disabled mode preserves original text.

### Actual

The proof and tests match the expected behavior.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Live speech-provider output for enabled, disabled, stream, telephony, and empty-after-strip paths.
- Focused speech-core test output.
- Focused TTS tool test output.
- Test and production typecheck output.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: enabled/disabled filtering, file synthesis, stream synthesis, telephony synthesis, empty-after-strip guard, adjacent TTS tool behavior.
- Edge cases checked: complete emoji/symbol sequences, emoji-only input, telephony provider request text, stream provider no-call behavior.
- Not verified: live vendor-specific pronunciation across every external provider.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: optional new config `messages.tts.skipEmojiSymbols`; no change required when unset.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Unicode stripping removes characters a user wanted spoken.
- Mitigation: behavior is opt-in and disabled by default.
- Risk: filtering differs across TTS paths.
- Mitigation: shared preparation is used before file, stream, and telephony synthesis, with regression tests for each path.

target.title: Audit config backups for plaintext secrets
target.body:
## Summary

/claim #11829

This closes a concrete remaining gap from the API-key protection roadmap: stale adjacent `openclaw.json` backups and managed snapshots can still contain plaintext credentials after the active config has moved to SecretRefs. `openclaw secrets audit` now scans those adjacent config artifacts and reports plaintext credential targets found there.

## Changes

- Add `listConfigBackupPaths()` coverage for rotation backups plus managed adjacent snapshots: `openclaw.json.bak`, `openclaw.json.bak.*`, `openclaw.json.*.bak`, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.*`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.*`.
- Parse backup/snapshot files with config-compatible JSON5 semantics instead of strict `JSON.parse`.
- Report unreadable, oversized, or unparsable backup snapshots as audit findings instead of silently treating them as clean.
- Report plaintext findings with the backup/snapshot file path and JSON path so users can clean stale copies.
- Add regression coverage for `.bak`, managed adjacent snapshots, and JSON5-authored backups.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw secrets audit --json` reports plaintext provider secrets from adjacent config backup/snapshot files while the active config uses an env SecretRef.

Real environment tested: local source checkout on Node.js 22.22.0 with a temporary OpenClaw state directory at `$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw`. The active `openclaw.json` used `apiKey: { source: "env", provider: "default", id: "OPENAI_API_KEY" }`. Adjacent stale snapshots contained redacted plaintext keys in `models.providers.openai.apiKey`: `openclaw.json.bak` with JSON5 comments/trailing commas, `openclaw.json.pre-update`, `openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z`, and `openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
rm -rf tmp/cli-proof
mkdir -p tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw
node -e 'write active SecretRef config plus adjacent stale snapshots'
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw" \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json" \
OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-redacted-live-env-key" \
pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json
```

Evidence after fix: redacted terminal output from the real CLI command above:

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "status": "findings",
  "resolution": {
    "refsChecked": 1,
    "skippedExecRefs": 0,
    "resolvabilityComplete": true
  },
  "filesScanned": [
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
    "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z"
  ],
  "summary": {
    "plaintextCount": 4,
    "unresolvedRefCount": 0,
    "shadowedRefCount": 0,
    "legacyResidueCount": 0
  },
  "findings": [
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.bak",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.clobbered.20260228T010203Z",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.pre-update",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    },
    {
      "code": "PLAINTEXT_FOUND",
      "severity": "warn",
      "file": "$PWD/tmp/cli-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json.rejected.20260228T010203Z",
      "jsonPath": "models.providers.openai.apiKey",
      "message": "models.providers.openai.apiKey is stored as plaintext in a config backup.",
      "provider": "openai"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real CLI output shows `status: findings`, scans the active config plus all four adjacent backup/snapshot files, reports `plaintextCount: 4`, and emits one `PLAINTEXT_FOUND` finding for each stale adjacent config artifact. The active config SecretRef is resolvable (`refsChecked: 1`, `unresolvedRefCount: 0`).

What was not tested: I did not run a packaged npm install smoke for `openclaw secrets audit`; this proof uses the source-checkout CLI path via `pnpm exec tsx src/entry.ts secrets audit --json`. I also did not test Windows filesystem snapshot names manually beyond the repository test suite.

## Validation

pnpm exec vitest run src/secrets/audit.test.ts -t "adjacent config"

target.title: Warn on plaintext secret config in doctor
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a `doctor` security warning when `openclaw.json` contains plaintext secret-bearing fields such as `models.providers.<id>.apiKey` and sensitive provider headers like `Authorization`.
- Skip the warning for SecretRef-backed values so migrated configs stay quiet.
- Point users to `openclaw secrets configure` / `openclaw secrets apply` and `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

## Why

Issue #11829 tracks API key exposure to agents. The current secrets tooling can already audit and migrate plaintext values, but a normal `openclaw doctor` run can still look healthy while `openclaw.json` stores agent-readable API keys/tokens. This change makes that risk visible in the security warning surface users already run.

This does not claim to replace the broader secret-isolation architecture. It closes a practical visibility gap and helps users migrate away from plaintext config secrets before agents or workspace tools read them directly.

## Test

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/doctor-security.test.ts
```

Result: 1 test file passed, 30 tests passed.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` now surfaces plaintext secret-bearing model provider config in `openclaw.json`, including `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout after this patch, running `node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive` against a real temporary config file at `/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json`. The config contained redacted plaintext values in `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_HOME=/Users/lukaivanic/projects/organization/llm_money_making_experiment/folder_2/proof/openclaw-doctor-plaintext/home \
node openclaw.mjs doctor --non-interactive
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real CLI command above included this live output excerpt:

```text
◇  Security

  - WARNING: openclaw.json contains plaintext secret-bearing config
    fields.
    Paths: models.providers.openai.apiKey,
    models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization
    Agents or workspace tools that can read config files may see these
    API keys/tokens.
    Migrate them to SecretRefs with openclaw secrets configure or
    openclaw secrets apply, then verify with openclaw secrets audit
    --check.
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live doctor run printed a Security warning for both plaintext config paths: `models.providers.openai.apiKey` and `models.providers.openai.headers.Authorization`, and it pointed the user to SecretRefs plus `openclaw secrets audit --check`.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

Refs #11829

target.title: Doctor: expose dry-run preview reports
target.body:
## Goal

Expose the structured Doctor repair work through top-level dry-run, diff, and JSON preview reporting.

The earlier PRs in this stack migrate repair buckets so they can return structured `changes`, `warnings`, `effects`, and `diffs`. This PR wires that output into the user-facing preview surface with `doctor --dry-run`, `--diff`, and JSON reporting, while explicitly reporting unconverted repair contributions as skipped instead of pretending every Doctor repair is ready.

Notes are threaded through like the rest of OpenClaw: presentation only through `ctx.doctor.note(...)`; preview JSON does not include `detect().notes`, `repair().notes`, runner note arrays, or note-derived findings.

## Legacy To Modern Shape

Before this stack, Doctor repair output was mostly interactive presentation and command-local behavior. The migrated repair checks now share an isomorphic shape:

- `detect()` produces the findings used by lint, repair, dry-run, and post-repair validation
- `repair()` returns structured repair output for the selected findings
- `doctor --dry-run` can preview repair actions without mutation
- `--diff` can show file-backed repair diffs where available
- JSON preview can report findings, changes, warnings, effects, diffs, and skipped unconverted repairs

This PR is intentionally honest about partial migration: the preview surface only exposes structured output for converted contributions and records skipped entries for legacy repairs that still need a follow-on conversion.

## Converted Here

- top-level `doctor --dry-run`
- top-level `doctor --diff`
- JSON preview reporting for structured repairs
- skipped records for unconverted repair contributions
- tests for dry-run/diff/JSON preview behavior across the migrated repair contract

## Review Surface

This is the preview-surface PR to challenge:

- whether `doctor --dry-run` should be enabled with the current migrated majority or remain gated longer
- whether skipped unconverted repairs are reported clearly enough for users and CI
- whether JSON fields for findings, changes, warnings, effects, and diffs are stable enough for consumers
- whether `--diff` should require dry-run or be valid in additional modes
- whether notes are correctly excluded from machine-readable preview output

## Stack Position

Parent: #84450. This is the final PR in the current Doctor repair stack.

The stack builds in order:

| PR | Area |
| --- | --- |
| #83753 | base interactive maintenance repairs and repair contract refinement |
| #84290 | plugin registry and configured plugin installs |
| #84326 | sandbox repairs |
| #84340 | gateway service repairs |
| #84366 | session state repairs |
| #84450 | config audit and cron repairs |
| #84472 | dry-run, diff, and JSON preview surface |

## Verification

- Restacked on #84450 as a single commit.
- Code search confirmed this branch does not reintroduce `HealthPresentationNote`, `repairNote`, `detectionNotes`, `repairNotes`, preview note arrays, or structured note result surfaces.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Changed test coverage includes maintenance CLI registration, Doctor health preview, repair-flow behavior, and the converted domain repair tests carried through the stack.
- Fresh PR CI should provide broad proof across the full stack.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor` can expose structured dry-run, diff, and JSON preview output for migrated repair checks while reporting unconverted repair contributions as skipped.

Real environment tested: Windows checkout on the refreshed stack.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check`; no focused Vitest command completed locally for this PR body refresh.

Evidence after fix: The branch wires preview reporting into Doctor CLI/health flow tests and carries structured repair tests through the stack so dry-run, diff, JSON, effects, warnings, and skipped unconverted repairs are covered by the PR test surface.

Observed result after fix: Converted repairs can be previewed through structured output, notes remain presentation-only, and unconverted repairs are not silently represented as fully migrated.

What was not tested: Full platform-wide Doctor CI and live repair scenarios were not rerun locally for this body refresh.

target.title: fix: allow commands in groups when no allowFrom is configured
target.body:
## Problem

Slash commands (`/status`, `/new`, `/reset`) silently fail in group chats when no `allowFrom` list is configured. The bot shows no response at all.

## Root Cause

In `resolveCommandAuthorizedFromAuthorizers` (`src/channels/command-gating.ts`), when `useAccessGroups` is enabled (the default) and no `allowFrom` list exists, the authorizers array contains `{ configured: false, allowed: false }`.

The code `authorizers.some(e => e.configured && e.allowed)` returns `false` because `configured` is `false` — meaning "no allowlist was set up". This incorrectly denies ALL commands in groups without an explicit `allowFrom` list.

The `!useAccessGroups` path already handles this correctly:

```typescript
const anyConfigured = authorizers.some(e => e.configured);
if (!anyConfigured) return true; // No restrictions = allow
```

## Fix

Added the same `!anyConfigured` guard to the `useAccessGroups=true` path. Both paths now treat "no restrictions configured" as "allow" consistently.

**2 files changed, 26 insertions, 1 deletion.**

## Testing

- `command-gating.test.ts`: 10/10 pass
- Feishu bot test suite: 59/59 pass
- No behavioral change for groups with `allowFrom` configured

Fixes #49915

target.title: perf(plugins): cut plugin-system thrash from TUI cold start
target.body: big improvement to plugin loading performance on gateway AND TUI (only TUI local once #84686 is merged) BEFORE AFTER ## Summary Cuts plugin-system work during TUI cold start. Freeze drops from ~32s to ~13–16s; residual cost is dominated by two single-plugin module loads (Codex 2.76s, Anthropic 746ms) that this PR doesn't touch. The work is four core fixes, ranked by single-fix impact: ### 1. Cache-busting — snapshot memo store/lookup keys now match (~7s) Previously the memo computed its lookup key from the persisted index and stored entries under the derived index. Those indexes always differ in volatile fields (`generatedAtMs`, `refreshReason`), so the keys never agreed and every lookup missed. **Hit rate goes from 24% → 98%.** Single biggest commit in the PR. ### 2. Plumbing — `PluginMetadataSnapshot` routed through resolve paths (~2–4s) `resolvePluginProviders`, `isPluginProvidersLoadInFlight`, `resolveOwningPluginIdsForProvider`, and `resolveExternalAuthProfilesWithPlugins` all dispatch to helpers that have a manifestRegistry fast path. They now thread the snapshot through so the fast path actually fires instead of every helper rebuilding the manifest registry from disk. `loadPluginManifestRegistryForInstalledIndex` fires drop 685 → ~10. A second plumb threads `discovery?: PluginDiscoveryResult` through `applyPluginAutoEnable` → `detectPluginAutoEnableCandidates` → `resolvePluginAutoEnableReadiness` → `collectConfiguredChannelIds` → the bundled-channel leaf helpers, so a caller's discovery short-circuits the cascade. Only delivers value because (1) made the snapshot memo actually work. ### 3. LRU capacity (supporting) Memo was single-slot and thrashed when two stable input shapes alternated. Capacity-8 LRU keeps both. Necessary precondition for the cache-busting fix to land cleanly — without LRU, the memo evicts itself. ### 4. Eligibility predicate broadened (supporting) The memo previously only stored derived snapshots whose diagnostics were *exclusively* `persisted-registry-stale-policy`, which discarded ~98% of cold-start derives. Now stores every non-empty derived snapshot. To keep the invalidation surface honest, the cached entry's `watchedFiles` set is widened for derived snapshots to include the derived index's plugin manifest paths; the next lookup re-stats them and re-derives on any change. ## Verification - `pnpm tsgo --noEmit --project tsconfig.core.json` — clean - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/plugin-metadata-snapshot.memo.test.ts src/plugins/plugin-registry-snapshot.test.ts src/plugins/providers.test.ts` — 93/93 pass (includes a new regression for derived-only manifest edits) - `pnpm build && pnpm openclaw tui` from a cold process — freeze 32s → 13–16s, snapshot memo hit rate 24% → 98%, per-plugin `package.json` reads ~914 → ~370 Not run: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm check`, sibling-process plugin-install interleaving. ## Notes - Three `wip(perf-trace):` commits are preserved in history so the audit instrumentation is easy to cherry-pick for future startup work. The chore() commit at the tip reverts their effects, so net diff against main contains zero `perfMark`/`perfSpan` calls. - An `installRecords` threading optimization was pulled into [`dallin-perf-install-records-threading`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/tree/dallin-perf-install-records-threading) for a follow-on PR — it cut ~264 sync disk reads but only ~140ms of freeze, so not worth bundling here.

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): add resolve_exec_env hook for channel-specific env injection [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `resolve_exec_env` plugin hook allowing plugins to inject channel-specific environment variables into exec tool execution
- Hook fires after env construction in `bash-tools.exec.ts`, plugins return `Record<string, string>` which are merged into the exec environment
- Multiple plugins can contribute env vars; later plugins override earlier ones for the same key

## Motivation

Channel plugins (Feishu, Discord, Slack, Telegram, etc.) need to pass channel-specific env vars to exec commands (e.g. `FEISHU_APP_ID`, `DISCORD_GUILD_ID`). Currently this requires hardcoded references in core, violating the extension-agnostic principle. This hook enables any channel plugin to inject env vars generically.

| Channel | Example env vars |
|---------|-----------------|
| Feishu | `FEISHU_APP_ID`, `FEISHU_USER_ID`, `FEISHU_OPEN_ID` |
| Discord | `DISCORD_GUILD_ID`, `DISCORD_CHANNEL_ID` |
| Slack | `SLACK_WORKSPACE_ID`, `SLACK_CHANNEL_ID` |
| Telegram | `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID`, `TELEGRAM_USER_ID` |

## Test plan

- [x] Run `pnpm test src/plugins/hook-resolve-exec-env.test.ts` (4 new tests)
- [x] Verify hook-types compile-time assertion passes

target.title: fix(sessions): reduce session-store memory retention
target.body:
## Summary

This PR addresses the dominant session-store memory-retention issue observed in a high-load CodeClaw/OpenClaw triage deployment. It keeps `loadSessionStore()` compatibility intact, adds safer immutable read APIs, bounds serialized cache state, and migrates several read-only paths away from mutable full-store clones.

Changes:

- Intern repeated large `SessionEntry.skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings after session-store parse/clone.
- Add immutable session-store read APIs:
  - `readSessionStoreSnapshot(storePath)`
  - `readSessionEntry(storePath, sessionKey)`
  - `readSessionEntries(storePath)`
- Export the snapshot read APIs through plugin SDK/runtime session helpers.
- Bound `SESSION_STORE_SERIALIZED_CACHE` by store-path count and total bytes.
- Migrate safe read-only paths to snapshot/entry reads:
  - cron isolated delivery target lookup
  - delivery-info lookup
  - `readSessionUpdatedAt()`
  - auto-reply verbose progress refresh
  - cron timer delivery context lookup
  - subagent announce output token/stat lookup
- Isolate small agent-attempt lifecycle callbacks to reduce unnecessary active-run closure capture.

## Problem / RCA

I have been dealing with an unstable OpenClaw instance under a high load of sessions, tasks, and `sessions_spawn` activity. After several memory dump analyses, I traced the dominant issue to session-store clone amplification: repeated full-store reads were duplicating large `skillsSnapshot.prompt` strings across hundreds of retained session-store clones.

The important workload characteristic is not raw request volume; it is session-store shape plus repeated access. CodeClaw had accumulated hundreds of session entries, especially `agent:main` subagent/topic sessions, each carrying a large `skillsSnapshot.prompt`. Before this fix, every compatible `loadSessionStore()` clone parsed/cloned the full store and created fresh copies of those large prompt strings. Active runs and gateway/session paths then retained enough clones for V8 old-space to fill.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: reduce retained session-store clone/prompt memory in a real high-session OpenClaw deployment while preserving `loadSessionStore()` compatibility and adding immutable read APIs for safer read paths.
- Real environment tested: live `codeclaw-triage` container running OpenClaw/CodeClaw with triage cron enabled, real persistent session stores, and normal cron/subagent review activity.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: captured and analyzed pre-fix OOM/near-OOM heap snapshots, deployed the session-store fix stack into `codeclaw-triage`, let triage cron run for about 20 minutes with the real session registry loaded, then captured a fresh SIGUSR2 heap snapshot and analyzed exact prompt strings/session-store map retainers.
- Evidence after fix: copied live heap-analysis output from the CodeClaw triage container is below; artifacts are saved under `/tmp/openclaw-session-store-evidence/sigusr2-capture-20260520T171954Z`.

  ```text
  Pre-fix captured heaps reached 4.1-4.5 GB.
  First analyzed OOM heap:
    heap nodes: ~50.6M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: ~389,465
    exact skills prompt bytes: ~1.5 GiB
    full-store clone-like maps: ~925

  Post-fix live codeclaw-triage SIGUSR2 heap after ~20 min cron/subagent load:
    heap snapshot size: 387,801,524 bytes (~370 MiB)
    heap nodes: 3.63M
    exact skills prompt string nodes: 8
    exact skills prompt bytes: 55,312 bytes
    full-store clone-like maps: 25 fingerprint / 14 strong candidates
    container restart=0
    OOMKilled=false
    serialized session-cache strings: ~11.35 MiB under 64 MiB cap
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the container stayed healthy during validation (`restart=0`, `OOMKilled=false`), and the catastrophic `skillsSnapshot.prompt` clone multiplier was gone; serialized session-cache strings were ~11.35 MiB under the new 64 MiB default cap.
- What was not tested: this does not claim all OpenClaw memory pressure is gone; remaining pressure includes active-run state, task registry retention, serialized session-state strings, and normal Node/runtime caches. Further locking down/deprecating mutable full-store reads is left for follow-up API discussion.

## Compatibility / open question

This PR intentionally preserves `loadSessionStore()` as a mutable-clone compatibility API. The new snapshot APIs provide a safer read path, and this PR migrates several known hot read-only consumers, but existing callers and plugins can still call `loadSessionStore()`.

Open question for reviewers: should we keep this non-breaking compatibility path and migrate call sites gradually, or start a deprecation/guardrail plan for mutable full-store reads? The stricter long-term model would reserve mutable access for explicit writer/maintenance paths and require snapshot/entry APIs for reads.

## Validation

Worktree:

```text
/home/edpiva/repos/openclaw-worktrees/session-store-cache-read-paths
```

Commands/results:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/config/sessions.test.ts \
  src/config/sessions.cache.test.ts \
  src/config/sessions/store.skills-stripping.test.ts \
  src/config/sessions/store-read.test.ts \
  src/config/sessions/store-writer.test.ts \
  src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts \
  src/config/sessions/delivery-info.test.ts \
  src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.reply-dispatch.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.acp-abort.test.ts \
  src/cron/service/timer.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce.timeout.test.ts \
  src/agents/command/attempt-callbacks.test.ts \
  src/agents/auth-profile-runtime-contract.test.ts \
  --run

Test Files 21 passed

target.title: feat(diagnostics-otel): add per-message end-to-end OTel tracing across run/skill/tool/model
target.body:
﻿## Summary
- Problem: OpenClaw observability was too coarse to reliably trace one user message across the full execution chain (run -> skill -> tool -> model), making latency and failure attribution difficult.
- Why it matters: In private/self-hosted deployments (especially regulated environments), operators need per-message auditability and stage-level performance/error visibility in Jaeger/Prometheus.
- What changed: Added and standardized `message.processing.*` and `skill.execution.*` diagnostics events, aligned trace attributes (`run/session/message/chat` IDs + phase), and exported corresponding spans/metrics in `diagnostics-otel` for end-to-end stage analysis.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to model business logic, tool behavior semantics, auth flow, storage backend behavior, or UI product workflow.
## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra
## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra
## Linked Issue/PR
- Closes #<!-- fill if applicable -->
- Related #<!-- fill if applicable -->
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression
## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: Existing diagnostics coverage did not model message-level lifecycle and skill lifecycle as first-class, stage-aware events with consistent correlation attributes.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No standardized per-message trace contract (`message.processing.*`) and no dedicated skill lifecycle telemetry (`skill.execution.*`) with unified IDs/phases.
- Contributing context (if known): Trace parent-child timing/order and mixed attribute conventions across events reduced chain continuity and queryability.
## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts` (event schema/contract tests)
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` (emission sequence tests)
  - `extensions/diagnostics-otel/src/service.ts` (event -> span/metric mapping tests)
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - A single message run emits ordered `message.processing.started -> ... -> message.processing.completed/error`
  - Skill stages emit `skill.execution.started/completed/error` for `entries/env/prompt`
  - All relevant spans include unified IDs (`run_id`, `session_*`, `messageId`, `chatId`) and phase attributes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It validates the telemetry contract and export mapping directly at producer/consumer seams without requiring full E2E infra.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Partial existing coverage for run/tool/model diagnostics lifecycle.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (recommend adding/expanding seam tests in follow-up if not already present in branch).
## User-visible / Behavior Changes
- Jaeger now shows per-message lifecycle spans (`openclaw.message.processing`) with stage outcomes.
- Skill lifecycle is now observable via `openclaw.skill.execution` spans and metrics.
- New/standardized attributes for filtering and joins:
  - `openclaw.run_id`
  - `openclaw.session_key`
  - `openclaw.session_id`
  - `openclaw.messageId`
  - `openclaw.chatId`
  - `openclaw.message.phase`
  - `openclaw.skill.phase`
- New/expanded metrics for stage latency/outcome analysis in Prometheus:
  - `openclaw.message.processing.duration_ms`
  - `openclaw.message.processing.total`
  - `openclaw.skill.execution.duration_ms`
  - `openclaw.skill.execution.total`
  - `openclaw.skill.loaded_count`
## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[user message] -> [run/tool/model telemetry scattered] -> [hard to correlate per message]
After:
[user message]
  -> [message.processing.started]
  -> [run.started]
  -> [skill.execution: entries/env/prompt]
  -> [tool.execution / model.call]
  -> [message.processing.completed|error]
  -> [run.completed]
  -> [Jaeger/Prometheus can query full chain by run/session/message/chat IDs]

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Operators could not reliably attribute latency and failures to a single user message across run, skill load, tool prep, and model calls in self-hosted OpenClaw with OTLP/Jaeger.
- Real environment tested: Windows 11 developer workstation, Node 22, local clone of this PR branch with default dev dependencies (pnpm install).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: pnpm install; pnpm openclaw doctor for a quick local sanity check; pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 on touched TS files; pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/followup-delivery.test.ts; pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-stage-timing.test.ts (smoke on embedded attempt runner); pnpm test src/plugins/contracts/extension-runtime-dependencies.contract.test.ts (workspace dependency contract).
- Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript (paths redacted). The important part is live openclaw CLI plus Vitest shard output showing green runs after the telemetry changes:
```
> pnpm openclaw doctor
(config load + doctor checks completed with exit code 0)

> pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-stage-timing.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```
- Observed result after fix: Local openclaw doctor succeeds; targeted Vitest shards above pass, confirming the embedded attempt path and reply plumbing still execute with the new diagnostic emissions wired through.
- What was not tested: Full gateway + live OTLP collector/Jaeger UI on this machine (no long-lived collector here); full checks-node-core-runtime-shared matrix is left to GitHub Actions.
