target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: build: suppress rolldown-plugin-dts CommonJS dts warnings from bundled zod locales
target.body:
## Why not the more invasive fixes

Up front, here's why this PR only filters the warning instead of changing the build:

- **Setting `dts: false` on the tsdown node build** would remove `rolldown-plugin-dts` from the JS bundle pass entirely (`tsdown.config.ts:nodeBuildConfig`). It plausibly works because `build:plugin-sdk:dts` (tsgo) is what actually emits the dts files we ship, but it changes a behavior that's been in place since the tsdown migration. Any downstream step or tooling quietly relying on the tsdown-side dts emission would silently break — and a clean `pnpm build` confirms tsdown is emitting ~2k `*.d.ts` files into `dist/` today, several of which are not the plugin-sdk surface, so the blast radius isn't obviously empty.
- **Externalizing zod for the dts pass only** is what the warning literally suggests, but tsdown's `deps.alwaysBundle` / `deps.neverBundle` / `inputOptions.external` apply uniformly to JS and dts. The JS path needs zod inlined per #78515 (otherwise globally-installed openclaw fails to resolve `zod/*` subpaths). There's no per-pass externalization knob on `rolldown-plugin-dts`, so doing this cleanly would require splitting the build into two graphs.

The warning is purely informational from the bundler's perspective — the dts output is still valid; rolldown-plugin-dts is just hedging on edge cases for `export = ...` style declarations. Filtering it matches the existing `onLog` pattern in this file for `PLUGIN_TIMINGS`, scoped `UNRESOLVED_IMPORT`, and the bottleneck/protobufjs `EVAL` warnings.

## When/why it started

Two upstream changes combined:

1. `0496063264` (2026-05-10) bumped `rolldown-plugin-dts` 0.23.2 → 0.25.0.
2. `94ac563399` (2026-05-20) bumped 0.25.0 → 0.25.1, which is the version that introduced the new `CommonJS dts syntax` warning in `rolldown-plugin-dts:fake-js` (see `isCjsDtsInputSyntax` — fires on `export = ...` and `import x = require(...)`).

Combined with `ea72414e1c` / #78515, which added `zod` and `zod/*` to `shouldAlwaysBundleDependency` so the dts pass walks `src/plugin-sdk/zod.ts` → `zod/v4/classic/external.d.cts` → `../locales/index.cjs` → every per-locale `*.d.cts` (all of which use `export = _default`), every full build now emits one warning per locale file.

## Summary

- Extend `isSuppressedLog` in `tsdown.config.ts` to drop `PLUGIN_WARNING` logs from `rolldown-plugin-dts:fake-js` whose message contains `uses CommonJS dts syntax`. Other warnings from the same plugin still pass through.
- Add `plugin?: string` to the log type and two tests in `src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts` (one that suppresses the zod-locale message, one that forwards an unrelated warning to the default handler).
- Changelog entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Verification

Both runs in a clean Codex worktree on the rebased branch (post `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, so `rolldown-plugin-dts@0.25.1` is on disk):

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` spam of `(rolldown-plugin-dts:fake-js plugin) /…/zod/v4/locales/*.d.cts uses CommonJS dts syntax. …`
- Real environment tested: local macOS Codex worktree, Node 22.22.0, pnpm 11.1.0
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `rm -rf dist dist-runtime && OPENCLAW_BUILD_VERBOSE=1 node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs 2>&1 | grep -c "CommonJS dts"` (verbose mode bypasses our `onLog` patch — reproduces upstream warnings)
  - `rm -rf dist dist-runtime && node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs 2>&1 | grep -c "CommonJS dts"` (normal build — filter active)
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit`
  - `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --noEmit`
  - `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs tsdown.config.ts src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Verbose run (bypasses filter): **52** `CommonJS dts` lines emitted, sample `(rolldown-plugin-dts:fake-js plugin) /…/zod/v4/locales/be.d.cts uses CommonJS dts syntax. …`
  - Filtered run: **0** `CommonJS dts` lines; 4931 files in `dist/` — build still succeeds end-to-end
  - Vitest: 15 passed / 15 in `src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts` (13 existing + 2 new)
  - tsgo core + core-test: exit 0
  - oxlint: 0 warnings / 0 errors
- Observed result after fix: warnings gone from non-verbose `pnpm build`; legitimate other warnings remain reachable through the same `onLog` path.
- What was not tested: full `pnpm build` end-to-end past the `tsdown` step (postbuild, plugin-sdk:dts, etc.) — only the `tsdown` step is touched and only the log path changes; broader CI gates can confirm.

## Test plan

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts` — passes
- [x] Verbose vs filtered tsdown comparison shows 52 → 0
- [x] `tsgo` core + core-test typecheck clean
- [x] `oxlint` clean
- [ ] CI green

target.title: fix gateway restart outside systemd
target.body:
Closes #72223

## Summary
- detect Linux background gateway listeners via native `/proc` inspection before falling back to `lsof` and `ss`
- treat bare `openclaw-gateway` process titles as verified gateway processes for unmanaged restart signaling
- reuse resolved gateway probe auth during unmanaged restart health checks so auth-protected gateways can report healthy after SIGUSR1 restarts

## Testing
- pnpm test src/cli/daemon-cli/lifecycle.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/restart-health.test.ts
- pnpm test src/infra/gateway-process-argv.test.ts src/infra/gateway-processes.test.ts src/infra/restart-stale-pids.test.ts src/infra/restart.test.ts
- pnpm build
- verified locally in a non-systemd container: `node openclaw.mjs gateway restart --json` now returns `result: "restarted"` while the gateway is running in the background

target.title: Policy: add tool metadata conformance and runtime checks
target.body:
# Policy: add tool metadata conformance and runtime checks

## Summary

This PR extends the bundled Policy plugin from channel conformance into governed tool metadata. It keeps policy as an authored conformance layer over existing OpenClaw surfaces: `policy.jsonc` states requirements, `TOOLS.md` is observed as evidence, doctor/policy checks report drift, and the optional runtime gate consumes the same evidence when explicitly enabled.

- Adds `tools.requireMetadata` policy support for `risk`, `sensitivity`, and `owner`.
- Observes `TOOLS.md` declarations as tool evidence, including source `oc://TOOLS.md/tools/<id>` references.
- Adds doctor/policy findings for missing or unknown tool metadata.
- Keeps `policy check` and `doctor --lint` read-only.
- Adds opt-in `plugins.entries.policy.config.runtimeToolPolicy` enforcement through the existing trusted tool policy hook.
- Persists structured plugin approval metadata through `plugin.approval.request` so policy hash, evidence hash, and target references survive the gateway approval boundary.

## Quick Start

Enable the bundled Policy plugin and author a policy requirement:

```bash
openclaw plugins enable policy
```

```jsonc
{
  "tools": {
    "requireMetadata": ["risk", "sensitivity", "owner"],
  },
}
```

A matching `TOOLS.md` declaration should carry the required metadata, for example:

```md
## deploy
risk: critical
sensitivity: restricted
owner: ops
```

Run policy-only checks during authoring:

```bash
openclaw policy check
openclaw policy check --json
```

The same findings are available through `openclaw doctor --lint` when the Policy plugin is enabled.

## Configure Policy

Policy config remains under `plugins.entries.policy.config`:

```jsonc
{
  "plugins": {
    "entries": {
      "policy": {
        "enabled": true,
        "config": {
          "enabled": true,
          "path": "policy.jsonc",
          "runtimeToolPolicy": false,
          "workspaceRepairs": false,
          "expectedHash": "sha256:...",
          "expectedAttestationHash": "sha256:...",
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

`runtimeToolPolicy` is config-owned rather than policy-owned so deleting or breaking `policy.jsonc` cannot silently disable the runtime gate after the operator has opted in.

## Accept Policy State

`policy check --json` emits the stable audit tuple:

```text
policy hash + evidence hash + findings hash + clean result -> attestation hash
```

The tool evidence is part of the workspace evidence hash. A clean result can be accepted by recording `attestation.policy.hash` as `expectedHash` and `attestation.attestationHash` as `expectedAttestationHash`.

When runtime tool policy requests approval, the approval metadata now preserves the same audit values separately from the human-readable approval message:

```json
{
  "policyHash": "sha256:...",
  "evidenceHash": "sha256:...",
  "attestationHash": "sha256:...",
  "target": "oc://TOOLS.md/tools/deploy"
}
```

## Findings

This PR adds these policy findings:

| Finding | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| `policy/tools-missing-owner` | A governed tool declaration is missing owner metadata. |
| `policy/tools-missing-risk-level` | A governed tool declaration is missing risk metadata. |
| `policy/tools-unknown-risk-level` | A governed tool declaration uses an unknown risk value. |
| `policy/tools-missing-sensitivity-token` | A governed tool declaration is missing sensitivity metadata. |
| `policy/tools-unknown-sensitivity-token` | A governed tool declaration uses an unknown sensitivity value. |

Findings identify both sides of the decision: `target` points to the observed `TOOLS.md` declaration and `requirement` points to the authored policy requirement.

## Runtime Gate

`doctor --lint` and `policy check` remain read-only. `doctor --fix` does not edit `TOOLS.md` in this PR.

When `runtimeToolPolicy` is enabled, the Policy plugin registers a trusted tool policy. It:

- blocks tool calls if the enabled policy artifact is missing, invalid, or hash-mismatched;
- blocks governed tools whose required metadata is missing or invalid;
- asks for approval for governed tools marked `risk: critical` or `IRREVERSIBLE_EXTERNAL`;
- includes structured approval metadata with policy/evidence/attestation values and the target tool reference;
- otherwise lets the normal tool call path continue.

This PR does not add new approval UI, supervisor protocol, or gateway protocol concepts beyond preserving plugin approval metadata already produced by the runtime policy.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: With the Policy base now merged through #80407, this PR adds tool metadata conformance on top of the bundled Policy plugin, including `TOOLS.md` evidence, five tool finding IDs, opt-in `runtimeToolPolicy` enforcement, and structured approval metadata persistence through `plugin.approval.request`.

Real environment tested: WSL source checkout on current PR head `ae9cd92694fddbdcb2037fa833dbc8d78573edc3`, compared against current `origin/main` `a13468320c63573917c185db278f3d4e13389a78` after #80407 merged.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/runtime-tool-policy.test.ts --reporter=dot` (3 files / 45 tests, including unparseable `policy check --json` regression)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/flows/bundled-health-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot` (3 files / 63 tests)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/extension-import-boundaries.test.ts --reporter=dot` (1 file / 6 tests)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/oc-path/src/oc-path/tests/parse.test.ts extensions/oc-path/src/oc-path/tests/scenarios/h2-block-split.test.ts --reporter=dot` (2 files / 34 tests)
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-extensions-policy-oc-path-api.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/loader.test.ts -t 'writes bundled oc-path package aliases' --reporter=dot` (1 test, proves packaged dist `@openclaw/oc-path/api.js` alias creation)
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-extensions-policy-oc-path-alias.tsbuildinfo`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/runtime-tool-policy.test.ts test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts test/extension-import-boundaries.test.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts -t 'writes bundled oc-path package aliases' --reporter=dot"` (clean: no accepted/actionable findings)
- deterministic before/after source probe recorded in `before.json`, `after.json`, and `summary.txt` on the proof artifact branch

Evidence after fix: Focused tests passed for policy CLI, including unparseable `policy check --json`, policy doctor registration, runtime tool policy, bundled health check loading, plugin approval gateway handling, extension import boundaries, oc-path Markdown H2 parsing, extension type contracts, and whitespace checks. The refreshed artifact shows `origin/main` has the merged Policy plugin/channel base but no tool metadata checks, no runtime tool policy, and no approval metadata schema, while PR head has all five registered tool finding IDs, docs rows for all five, runtime missing/unknown metadata blocks, approval-path metadata tests, gateway approval metadata persistence/broadcast coverage, and multiline `TOOLS.md` metadata parsing.

Observed result after fix: PR #80056 keeps legacy channel-only evidence hashes stable when no `TOOLS.md` evidence is scanned, registers policy tool checks, records tool evidence with `oc://TOOLS.md/tools/<id>` targets, parses both inline and documented multiline metadata under `### <tool>` headings, emits tool metadata findings with target/requirement references, requests approval for multiline `risk: critical` declarations in runtime policy mode, and preserves policy approval metadata across `plugin.approval.request`. Policy now imports `parseMd` from `@openclaw/oc-path/api.js` for Markdown H2 block parsing and line offsets; policy still owns the tool metadata grammar.

Current-head deterministic proof artifact:

![Fresh current-head proof for policy tool metadata, runtime gate, multiline metadata, and approval metadata](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80056-fresh/pr-80056/pr-80056-policy-tool-metadata-runtime-before-after-proof.png)

Supporting artifact files:

- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80056-fresh/pr-80056/summary.txt
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80056-fresh/pr-80056/before.json
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-80056-fresh/pr-80056/after.json

What was not tested: no new approval UI, supervisor protocol, or gateway protocol UI surface; those remain intentionally out of scope for this PR. Full docs formatter currently reports an unrelated `docs/reference/templates/CLAUDE.md` formatting issue on this checkout; the policy docs table was formatted and the PR-specific docs finding is fixed.

## Related

- Channels-first policy conformance: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80407
- Model, network, and MCP conformance examples: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80783
- Runtime audit metadata and attestation enforcement: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81104

<!-- policy-stack-links:start -->

## Policy stack links

This PR is part of the Policy 1.0 proof stack. Final category branches added after the runtime-audit layer:

- #81974 — Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks
- #81981 — Policy: add gateway exposure checks

Full stack reference:

1. #80407 — Policy: add conformance system with channel checks
2. #80056 — Policy: add tool metadata conformance and runtime checks
3. #80783 — Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
4. #81104 — Policy: add runtime audit metadata and attestation enforcement
5. #81974 — Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks
6. #81981 — Policy: add gateway exposure checks

<!-- policy-stack-links:end -->

target.title: feat(auth): add models auth clean command to prune stale auth profiles
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `openclaw models auth clean` command that identifies and removes stale auth profiles no longer referenced by provider config
- Supports `--dry-run`, `--json`, and `--agent` flags
- Uses file locking to prevent concurrent write races
- Sanitizes profile IDs to prevent terminal injection in diagnostic output
- Uses atomic temp-file-then-rename writes in saveJsonFile (CWE-377 fix)
- Adds `loadAgentLocalAuthProfileStore` to prevent credential scope bleed when cleaning subagent stores

Supersedes #49429, #54171, #41640 (rebased onto current main v2026.4.14).

## Motivation

Stale auth profiles accumulate over time (expired OAuth tokens, removed providers, orphaned external CLI syncs). These stale entries cause recurring "Model login failed" errors when the sync mechanism overwrites fresh credentials with expired ones from external CLI files like `~/.codex/auth.json`.

## Test plan

- [x] `src/commands/models/auth-clean.test.ts` -- 35 tests
- [x] `src/agents/auth-profiles/store.legacy-migration.test.ts` -- 6 tests
- [x] `src/cli/models-cli.test.ts` -- 4 tests
- [x] `pnpm tsgo` passes
- [x] `pnpm build` passes

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `openclaw models auth clean` prunes profiles present in the agent's `auth-profiles.json` store that are no longer referenced by `openclaw.json` (`auth.profiles` / `auth.order`), keeping configured profiles. Previously there was no command to remove orphaned/stale store entries.
- **Real environment tested**: local `openclaw` build (current upstream/main + this PR) against an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` with a disposable store containing one CONFIGURED profile (`anthropic:keep-me`, present in `openclaw.json` auth.profiles) and one ORPHANED profile (`openai-codex:stale-orphan`, present only in the store).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # openclaw.json auth.profiles = { "anthropic:keep-me": {...} }
  # store auth-profiles.json profiles = { anthropic:keep-me, openai-codex:stale-orphan }
  OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/authclean-home node dist/index.js models auth clean --dry-run
  OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/authclean-home node dist/index.js models auth clean
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: captured live from `node dist/index.js models auth clean`:

  BEFORE — store profile ids:
  ```
  ['anthropic:keep-me', 'openai-codex:stale-orphan']
  ```
  DRY-RUN — previews the prune without writing:
  ```
  Configured: anthropic:keep-me
  In store:   anthropic:keep-me, openai-codex:stale-orphan
  Profiles to remove (1):
    - openai-codex:stale-orphan
  Profiles to keep (1):
    + anthropic:keep-me
  (dry run -- no changes written)
  ```
  REAL RUN — prunes the orphan:
  ```
  Profiles to remove (1):
    - openai-codex:stale-orphan
  Profiles to keep (1):
    + anthropic:keep-me
  Removed 1 stale profile(s). Restart the gateway to apply.
  ```
  AFTER — store profile ids (orphan pruned, configured retained):
  ```
  ['anthropic:keep-me']
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: the orphaned `openai-codex:stale-orphan` profile is removed from the store while the configured `anthropic:keep-me` profile is kept. Dry-run previews identically without writing. A timestamped backup of the prior store is written before the rewrite.
- **What was not tested**: pruning against a live production store (used a disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME` to avoid touching real credentials). The `--force` path for a store with zero configured profiles (covered by unit tests; not exercised here since the realistic case keeps at least one configured profile).

target.title: fix(session): prefer real tool result over synthetic error in transcript repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix transcript repair to prefer real tool results over synthetic "missing tool result" errors during deduplication.

When a channel (e.g. Feishu) has a flush-race condition, the session-tool-result-guard may write a synthetic error result before the real result arrives from the channel. On the next session load, `repairToolUseResultPairing` deduplicates by keeping the first-seen result — which is the synthetic error. The user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call actually succeeded.

This PR adds `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detection and replaces an existing synthetic result with a later real result during deduplication.

> **Note:** This is a transcript-repair mitigation. The underlying flush-race in the session-tool-result-guard (#84134) is a separate root-cause concern tracked independently.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `session-transcript-repair.ts` | Add `isSyntheticMissingToolResult()` detector; track output positions; replace synthetic with real during dedup |
| `session-transcript-repair.test.ts` | 5 regression tests: synthetic-first, real-first, two-real, two-synthetic, span-level replacement |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast ../../src/agents/session-transcript-repair.test.ts (40 tests) 5ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a synthetic "missing tool result" error is persisted before the real result arrives (Feishu channel flush race), the repair pass keeps the synthetic error and the user sees "Something went wrong" even though the tool call succeeded.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, running the actual `repairToolUseResultPairing` function from the source tree against a realistic Feishu session scenario.

### Failing proof before fix (main branch)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on main branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)
  Scenario: Feishu flush race — synthetic error persisted before real result arrived

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2
  Kept result isError: true
  Kept result text: "[openclaw] missing tool result in session history; ..."

❌ FAIL: Synthetic error was kept
   → User sees "Something went wrong" even though message was delivered
```

### Passing proof after fix (this PR)

```
$ npx tsx proof-84419-runner.mts  # on fix branch

--- Input: session with synthetic-first, real-second tool result ---
  Messages: 4
  Tool results: 2 (1 synthetic error, 1 real success)

--- Output: after repair ---
  Messages: 3
  Tool results: 1
  Kept result isError: false
  Kept result text: "Message sent successfully to Feishu user feishu_user_123"

✅ PASS: Real result preferred over synthetic error
   → User sees successful delivery, not "Something went wrong"

--- Scenario 2: real-first, synthetic-second ---
  Kept result isError: false
✅ PASS: Real result kept (synthetic duplicate dropped)
```

- **Evidence after fix**: The repair function correctly identifies synthetic "missing tool result" markers and replaces them with real results when both exist for the same tool call ID. Both orderings (synthetic-first and real-first) produce the correct outcome.
- **What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel reproduction (requires a configured Feishu bot with the specific flush-race timing). The underlying flush race in `session-tool-result-guard` (#84134) is not addressed by this PR — it mitigates the downstream effect in the transcript repair pass.

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched (shared gate functions — model-capability resolution, OAuth label, credential fingerprint, live-agent probes, model alias detection — extended via a single `isClaudeCliCompatibleBackend(provider)` helper to recognise both backend ids; every gate continues to return identical values for `claude-cli`). `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(feishu): disable ambient proxy inheritance for websocket by default
target.body:
## Summary

- Disable Feishu WebSocket ambient proxy inheritance by default.
- Add `OPENCLAW_FEISHU_WS_USE_PROXY=1` as the explicit opt-in for Feishu WS proxy routing.
- Cover default/no-proxy and opt-in proxy behavior in Feishu client tests.

Closes #65799.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Feishu WebSocket should not inherit ambient `HTTPS_PROXY` / `https_proxy` by default. It should only use ambient proxy env when `OPENCLAW_FEISHU_WS_USE_PROXY=1`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS local OpenClaw worktree, real `@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk` `WSClient`, real `@openclaw/proxyline` ambient proxy agent, local TLS `wss://` server, local HTTP proxy recording `CONNECT`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local proof script that starts a temporary `wss://127.0.0.1:<port>` WebSocket server and temporary HTTP proxy, sets `https_proxy=http://127.0.0.1:<proxyPort>`, then connects once without a WS agent and once with the ambient proxy agent.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
default ambient env, no ws agent:
  stateAfterReady: connected
  wsConnectionsDelta: 1
  proxyEvents: []
  agentConstructed: false

explicit opt-in agent from ambient env:
  stateAfterReady: connected
  wsConnectionsDelta: 1
  proxyEvents:
    CONNECT 127.0.0.1:<port>
  agentConstructed: true
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Ambient proxy env no longer affects Feishu WS by default; explicit opt-in routes the same WS connection through the proxy.
- **What was not tested:** No live Feishu production app credentials were used; the proof isolates the WebSocket/proxy transport behavior with a local TLS WS endpoint.

## Verification

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  16 passed (16)
```

```text
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/feishu/src/client.ts extensions/feishu/src/client.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p extensions/feishu/tsconfig.json --noEmit
```

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: feat: Update Tailscale Gateway w/ custom control server configuration, validation & test coverage
target.body:
- Introduced `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` for connecting to self-hosted Tailscale-compatible servers like Headscale.
- Updated validation to reject `serve` and `funnel` modes when a custom control server is specified.
- Added tests to ensure correct behavior of the new configuration options and validation rules.
- Updated documentation to reflect changes in Tailscale integration and usage with custom control servers.

Closes #48367

## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw’s Tailscale Gateway flow is currently optimized for the default Tailscale control plane (`serve`/`funnel` workflows).
- Why it matters: Self-hosted users running Headscale must point the Tailscale client to a custom control server (`tailscale up --login-server ...`) and run the Gateway in a compatible mode.
- Fix: add `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` and `--tailscale-control-url` so custom control servers are explicit and validated.
- Safety: reject unsupported combinations (`controlUrl` + `tailscale.mode=serve|funnel`) with actionable errors.
- Coverage/docs: add focused validation/runtime tests and update Tailscale Gateway docs with Headscale usage and limits.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #48367

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config key: `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` (URL).
- New CLI flag: `--tailscale-control-url <url>`.
- Startup/validation now fails fast when `controlUrl` is combined with `tailscale.mode: "serve"` or `"funnel"`.
- URL format is validated for `controlUrl`; invalid values are rejected with clear errors.
- Docs now include Headscale-specific setup and explicit compatibility limits.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)  
  - Adds ability to target a custom Tailscale-compatible control server configuration.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)  
  - No new OpenClaw network client path added; this is config/validation/runtime gating.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)  
  - New CLI option `--tailscale-control-url`.
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Risk: misconfiguration could lead to unsupported mode selection.
  - Mitigation: hard validation blocks unsupported combinations and returns actionable guidance (`mode=off` + `bind=tailnet`).

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS (darwin)
- Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway + Tailscale (Headscale-compatible control server)
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `gateway.bind: "tailnet"`
  - `gateway.auth.mode: "token"`
  - `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl: "https://headscale.example.com"`

### Steps

1. Configure `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` with `tailscale.mode: "off"` (or unset mode) and start gateway.
2. Set `tailscale.mode: "serve"` (then `"funnel"`) with the same `controlUrl` and validate/start again.
3. Pass `--tailscale-control-url not-a-url` and run gateway command.

### Expected

- `controlUrl` + mode `off` (or unset) is accepted.
- `controlUrl` + `serve|funnel` is rejected with clear compatibility error.
- Invalid URL input is rejected immediately.

### Actual

- Matches expected behavior via validation/runtime logic and dedicated tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Evidence references:
- `src/config/tailscale-control-url-validation.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-runtime-config.test.ts` (`tailscale.controlUrl` cases)
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts` (CLI URL validation and override wiring)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `controlUrl` accepted with `mode=off` and with mode omitted.
  - `controlUrl` rejected with `mode=serve|funnel`.
  - invalid `--tailscale-control-url` rejected by CLI.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `serve`/`funnel` behavior unchanged when `controlUrl` is not set.
  - Config + CLI override paths both enforce URL validity.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Live end-to-end against a real Headscale deployment in this change set.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Optional adoption only: set `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` or pass `--tailscale-control-url`.
  - If using custom control server, use `tailscale.mode: "off"` and `gateway.bind: "tailnet"`.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Remove `gateway.tailscale.controlUrl` and run prior/default Tailscale flow.
  - Or keep `controlUrl` and force `tailscale.mode: "off"` with `gateway.bind: "tailnet"`.
- Files/config to restore:
  - Gateway config `gateway.tailscale.*` and CLI invocation flags.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - Startup error mentioning custom control server incompatibility with `serve`/`funnel`.
  - CLI error for invalid `--tailscale-control-url`.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Users expect `serve`/`funnel` to work with Headscale and view rejection as regression.
  - Mitigation: explicit docs, clear error messages, and recommended supported path (`mode=off` + `bind=tailnet`).
- Risk: Slight DX confusion between Tailscale client login server config and OpenClaw gateway config.
  - Mitigation: docs include both steps and example commands for each layer.

target.title: feat: configure metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, etc.) for custom provider setup
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw configure --section model` sets up providers and auth but never prompts for model metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, reasoning, input types, cost). Users must hand-edit config or run many `config set` calls to configure these. - Why it matters: Custom/self-hosted endpoints default to contextWindow=16000 and maxTokens=4096, which are often wrong. Users have no interactive way to correct this during setup. - What changed: After custom provider setup in `promptAuthConfig`, the wizard now asks "Configure metadata for `/`?" and walks through the core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields (identity, capabilities, limits, cost) with current values prefilled. Built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) are unaffected. Their catalog already provides defaults. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing provider/auth flow is untouched. The metadata prompt is purely additive (appended after the custom provider path only). No config schema changes. No new CLI commands. Declining the prompt preserves the previous behavior exactly. Built-in provider flows are not modified. Compat flags (supportsStore, supportsTools, etc.) are not prompted for; they use sensible runtime defaults when absent and can be set manually in config by advanced users. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw configure --section model` with a **custom provider**: after provider setup completes, a new confirm prompt appears: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)". Answering Yes walks through all model metadata fields. Answering No preserves previous behavior. - **Built-in providers** (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.): no change. The metadata prompt does not appear. - Fields prompted (custom only): display name, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, context window, max output tokens, token pricing (gated behind its own confirm). - All fields prefilled with current values; pressing Enter keeps defaults. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0) - Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm - Model/provider: Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint ([thegrid.ai](https://thegrid.ai/)) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): Standard `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with custom provider ### Steps 1. Run `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` 2. Select "Custom Provider" and complete auth/model setup 3. Observe the new prompt: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)" 4. Answer Yes and walk through the fields, or answer No to skip 5. (Optional) Repeat with a built-in provider (e.g. OpenAI) and confirm the metadata prompt does **not** appear ### Expected - After custom provider setup, a metadata configuration prompt appears - Answering Yes allows editing all core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields - Answering No skips metadata editing (same behavior as before this change) - Updated metadata is written to `models.providers..models[]` in config - Built-in providers skip the metadata prompt entirely ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 18 unit tests in `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` covering: `collectConfiguredModels`, `findModelInConfig`, `findPrimaryModelEntry`, `applyModelDefinitionUpdate`, `formatTokenCount`, `promptModelMetadata`, `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` (including skip for built-in providers). All passing. Existing `configure.wizard.test.ts` tests also pass. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Ran `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` end-to-end with a custom provider (thegrid-ai), confirmed metadata prompt appears after model setup. Ran with OpenAI, confirmed metadata prompt does **not** appear. - Edge cases checked: Declining metadata prompt preserves config unchanged; missing primary model gracefully skips; provider with no models returns empty list; built-in providers silently skip the prompt - What you did **not** verify: All built-in provider auth paths (only tested OpenAI). Other built-in providers use the same `authChoice !== "custom-api-key"` code path so are expected to behave identically. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes`: declining the new prompt produces identical config to before - Config/env changes? `No`: writes to existing `models.providers.*.models[]` fields - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the three-line addition in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (lines 138-140: the `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` block calling `promptModelMetadataForPrimary`) - Files/config to restore: `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (remove import + call), delete `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` and `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If the metadata prompt throws, it would interrupt the configure wizard after custom provider setup but before config write. The `WizardCancelledError` path is handled by the parent wizard. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Metadata prompt adds extra steps to the custom provider configure flow, potentially annoying for users who don't need it. - Mitigation: Gated behind a confirm ("Configure metadata?") defaulting to Yes but skippable with a single keypress. Cost is further gated behind its own confirm.

target.title: fix(pdf): resolve custom provider models with config-aware resolver
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix the `pdf` tool so config-defined custom provider models continue to resolve when the runtime registry misses.
- Preserve the existing normalized registry lookup semantics before falling back to `models.providers`.
- Match configured inline provider models across normalized provider aliases, including provider-scoped model ids.
- Add regression coverage and a changelog entry for the user-visible PDF fix.

## What changed

- In `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`, switch the PDF model lookup from registry-only resolution to `resolveModelFromRegistryOrConfig(...)`.
- In `src/agents/tools/media-tool-shared.ts`, make the new resolver first reuse the existing normalized registry resolver, then fall back to configured inline provider models.
- In `src/agents/tools/media-tool-shared.test.ts`, cover config fallback plus alias-backed provider matching such as `aws-bedrock` -> `amazon-bedrock`.
- In `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`, cover the PDF execution path when a configured custom provider model resolves after a registry miss.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The `pdf` tool failed with `Unknown model: local-openai/gpt-5.4-mini` when `agents.defaults.pdfModel.primary` pointed at a custom provider model defined only under `models.providers`.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw `2026.4.10` on Manjaro Linux with a real local OpenClaw gateway, a local OpenAI-compatible provider configured under `models.providers.local-openai`, and `agents.defaults.pdfModel.primary = local-openai/gpt-5.4-mini`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Applied the local runtime-equivalent resolver fix, restarted the real gateway, and sent the same `pdf` tool request that previously failed against the configured `local-openai/gpt-5.4-mini` route.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the real gateway `pdf` request after the fix:

```text
This PDF contains only the text “Dummy PDF file” and no other content.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The same configured custom provider PDF route executed successfully instead of failing during model resolution, proving the failure was in the PDF model-resolution path rather than PDF extraction or provider compatibility.
- **What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

## Verification

Ran locally on this rebased PR branch:

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/tools/media-tool-shared.test.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tools/media-tool-shared.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts src/agents/tools/media-tool-shared.test.ts src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts CHANGELOG.md
pnpm check:changed
```

Results:

- `2 passed` test files, `30 passed` tests.
- All matched files use the correct format.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed after running typecheck, lint, import-cycle, conflict-marker, changelog-attribution, and related changed-surface guards.

## Linked issue

- Closes #65742

target.title: Strip leaked reasoning preambles before outbound send
target.body:
## Summary

This PR strips leaked plain-text reasoning preambles before outbound assistant replies are delivered to user-facing channels.

The current patch is intentionally narrow:

- routes delivery text through the canonical assistant-visible sanitizer from `sanitizeUserFacingText`
- strips high-confidence untagged reasoning preambles such as `I should...` and explicit `Let me think...` / `Let me reason...`
- preserves ordinary user-facing prose, including `Let me show...`, `The user...` summaries, fenced/schema examples, and unfenced CSS snippets
- does not reintroduce structural contamination, CSS, or footer cleanup into the delivery sanitizer

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Regression test

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime / reply delivery
- [x] Shared text sanitization
- [x] Tests

## Linked Issue/PR

Related to leaked reasoning preambles reaching Telegram and other outbound delivery surfaces.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The production delivery path uses `sanitizeUserFacingText`, while the earlier preamble cleaner was only present on `sanitizeAssistantVisibleText`. That left plain-text reasoning preambles visible in real outbound messages even when sanitizer tests passed against the wrong boundary.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

Focused local verification:

```bash
CI=true corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.sanitizeuserfacingtext.test.ts
```

Result on head `a5fe6aa1a328d28024498c9d57607445606b6655`:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       221 passed (221)
```

Additional sanity check:

```bash
git diff --check
```

Result: no whitespace errors.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

High-confidence leaked reasoning preambles are removed before delivery. Ordinary visible reply text that resembles documentation or instructional prose is preserved.

## Security Impact (required)

No new permissions, dependencies, workflow changes, network surfaces, or secret-handling paths are introduced.

The main risk is message-delivery compatibility: a sanitizer false positive could hide visible text, while a false negative could leave leaked preambles visible. The patch therefore keeps the heuristic narrow and adds preservation regressions for known false-positive shapes.

## Repro + Verification

The sanitizer is exercised through both:

- `sanitizeAssistantVisibleText`, the shared delivery profile
- `sanitizeUserFacingText`, the production-facing helper used before outbound delivery

Covered strip cases include:

- GLM-style planning lines paired with visible output
- `The user...` only when paired with explicit planning text
- `Let me think...` and `Let me reason...` reasoning preambles

Covered preservation cases include:

- ordinary `The user...` summaries
- ordinary `Let me show...` instructional prose
- first-person instructional prose
- unfenced CSS snippets
- fenced JSON schema examples

## Evidence

Local targeted tests pass on the current head.

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       221 passed (221)
```

Smoke output from the actual TypeScript sanitizer modules is included in the Real behavior proof section below.

## Human Verification (required)

I verified the focused sanitizer tests and direct smoke output locally. Live Telegram delivery from a deployed gateway on this exact PR head has not been captured yet; maintainers can request Mantis proof if transport-level confirmation is required.

## Review Conversations

Latest ClawSweeper feedback requested a narrow fix for explicit `let me think` / `let me reason` preambles without making generic `let me` prose strong evidence. This head implements that boundary and adds strip/preserve regressions.

## Compatibility / Migration

No migration required. The change affects outbound sanitizer behavior only.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **False positives:** Kept generic `let me`, `The user...`, CSS snippets, and schema examples preserved by tests.
- **False negatives:** Added targeted strong scoring for explicit reasoning phrases like `Let me think...` and `Let me reason...`.
- **Broad channel impact:** Kept the patch scoped to delivery-profile leaked-preamble cleanup only.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Leaked plain-text reasoning preambles are stripped through the user-facing delivery sanitizer, including explicit `Let me think...` / `Let me reason...` forms, while ordinary `Let me show...`, `The user...` summaries, and unfenced CSS snippets are preserved.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw workspace checkout on commit `a5fe6aa1a328d28024498c9d57607445606b6655`, using the actual TypeScript sanitizer modules through `corepack pnpm exec tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `corepack pnpm exec tsx` with direct imports from `sanitize-user-facing-text.ts` and `assistant-visible-text.ts`, invoking let-me-think, let-me-show, ordinary-user-summary, and unfenced-CSS cases.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the command above:

```json
{
  "letMeThink": "Visible answer.",
  "letMeShowKept": "Let me show you the setup.\n\nThen we can wire the deployment.",
  "userSummaryKept": "The user can configure the plugin from the settings screen.\nThe user should run the command again after saving.\n\nThis summary is user-facing and should remain intact.",
  "cssKept": "Use these styles:\ncolor: red;\nfont-family: sans-serif;\n\nThen reload the app."
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The explicit `Let me think...` reasoning preamble collapses to only `Visible answer.`; ordinary `Let me show...`, `The user...` summaries, and unfenced CSS snippets are unchanged.
- **Automated regression tests:** `CI=true corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.sanitizeuserfacingtext.test.ts` => 221 passed.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram delivery from a deployed gateway on this exact PR head; that still needs maintainer/Mantis proof or a live affected setup.

target.title: Honor OpenAI thinking profiles over stale catalog opt-outs
target.body:
## Summary

- Lets OpenAI/OpenAI-Codex provider-owned thinking profiles override stale catalog `reasoning: false` rows.
- Keeps non-OpenAI `reasoning: false` catalog rows as explicit off-only opt-outs.
- Adds resolver and `/think` directive regressions for `openai/gpt-5.5` and `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

Closes #84646.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
`/think xhigh` on canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` could be rejected with `Use one of: off` when stale runtime catalog metadata marked the model `reasoning: false`, even though the OpenAI provider profile advertises non-off thinking levels for that model.

Real environment tested:
Local macOS worktree with bundled Node.js `v24.14.0` and repository dependencies.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node node_modules/tsx/dist/cli.mjs /private/tmp/openclaw-84646-proof/proof.ts
PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.model.test.ts extensions/openai/openai-provider.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD
git log --format='%h %an <%ae> %s' upstream/main..HEAD
```

Evidence after fix:
The standalone runtime proof imports the real thinking resolver and asks for `openai/gpt-5.5` with a stale `reasoning:false` catalog row:
```json
{
  "openaiGpt55LevelsWithStaleFalse": [
    "off",
    "minimal",
    "low",
    "medium",
    "high",
    "xhigh"
  ],
  "openaiGpt55Formatted": "off, minimal, low, medium, high, xhigh",
  "openaiGpt55XHighSupported": true,
  "googleReasoningFalseLevels": [
    "off"
  ]
}
```

Targeted tests:
```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       123 passed (123)
```

Observed result after fix:
`openai/gpt-5.5` keeps the OpenAI provider thinking profile and accepts `xhigh` even when stale catalog metadata says `reasoning:false`. A separate Google `reasoning:false` catalog row remains off-only, preserving explicit non-OpenAI opt-out behavior.

What was not tested:
I did not run a live Telegram/Pi OAuth session or the full repository test suite. The proof covers the real thinking resolver plus targeted directive/runtime tests for the reported rejection path.

## Attribution

If this PR is squashed or reworked, please preserve the commit author attribution for Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com>, or include:

Co-authored-by: Andy Ye <35905412+TurboTheTurtle@users.noreply.github.com>
target.author: turbotheturtle

target.title: Cron: Add additional targets that result of cron jobs will be sent to multiple channels
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Cron jobs could only deliver their output to a single channel/recipient; there was no built-in way to fan-out the same result to multiple destinations simultaneously. Additionally, the Discord channel plugin was missing session-routing utilities needed for correct outbound session-key resolution, and `commands-session.ts` eagerly initialized the plugin runtime at module load time, which could crash at startup when channel plugins were not yet available.
- **Why it matters:** Teams that monitor activity across multiple channels (e.g. Telegram + Discord + Signal) had to create duplicate cron jobs for each destination, duplicating configuration and increasing scheduling overhead.
- **What changed:**
  - Added `additionalTargets` field to `CronDelivery` — an array of `{ channel, to, accountId? }` entries that receive the same payloads after the primary delivery succeeds. Each target is resolved and delivered independently; one failure does not block the others.
  - Added `deliverToAdditionalTargets()` in `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts` and wired it into the cron run pipeline in `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`.
  - Added normalization and validation of `additionalTargets` in `src/cron/normalize.ts` (trims/lowercases `channel`, trims `to`, strips blank `accountId`, filters out entries missing either required field).
  - Imported `buildAgentSessionKey`, `resolveThreadSessionKeys`, and `RoutePeer` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/routing` into `extensions/discord/src/channel.ts` to fix outbound session-key construction for Discord.
  - Changed `channelRuntime` in `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts` from an eager module-level constant to a lazy `getChannelRuntime()` accessor, preventing plugin runtime initialization at import time.
- **What did NOT change:** Primary delivery behavior, failure-destination logic, cron scheduling, and all other channel plugins are unmodified.


## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #

## AI/Vibe-Coded PRs
- [x] Mark as AI-assisted in the PR title or description
- [x] Note the degree of testing : fully tested
- [x] Confirm you understand what the code does: Yes

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

**New `delivery.additionalTargets` field for cron jobs.** After the primary delivery completes, the same output payloads are sent to each additional target. Example:

```json
"delivery": {
  "mode": "announce",
  "channel": "telegram",
  "to": "123456789",
  "additionalTargets": [
    { "channel": "signal", "to": "+15550001111" },
    { "channel": "discord", "to": "channel-456", "accountId": "bot-b" }
  ]
}
```

- Fan-out is best-effort per target: a failure on one target is logged and skipped; remaining targets still receive delivery.
- `bestEffort` from the primary delivery is inherited by all additional targets.
- Invalid entries (empty `channel` or `to`, blank `accountId`) are silently filtered at normalization time.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `Yes` — cron jobs can now deliver to additional channel targets, increasing the outbound delivery surface.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — each additional target results in an outbound messaging call using the existing `deliverOutboundPayloads` path, which already applies SSRF and channel-allowlist policies.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Fan-out reuses the existing `resolveDeliveryTarget` + `deliverOutboundPayloads` stack, so all existing allowlist, SSRF, and auth checks apply to each additional target identically. No new bypass paths are introduced.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / Bun
- Model/provider: any
- Integration/channel: Telegram, Signal, Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `delivery.additionalTargets` in a cron job

### Steps

1. Create a cron job with `delivery.mode = "announce"`, a primary `channel`/`to`, and an `additionalTargets` array containing two entries (e.g. Signal + Discord).
2. Trigger the job manually or wait for its scheduled run.
3. Confirm the primary channel receives the output.
4. Confirm each additional target also receives the same output.
5. Disable one additional target's channel to confirm the other target still receives delivery and the job does not fail.

### Expected

- Primary delivery succeeds.
- Each additional target independently receives the same payloads.
- A failing additional target logs a warning and does not affect other targets or the run outcome.

### Actual

- Matches expected in all three cases.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after — new unit tests in `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.additional-targets.test.ts` cover: successful fan-out, target-resolution failure isolation, delivery failure isolation, `accountId` forwarding, empty targets array, and `bestEffort` flag propagation.
- [x] Normalization tests in `src/cron/normalize.test.ts` cover: valid targets, casing/trimming, invalid-entry filtering, empty-array cleanup, and blank `accountId` stripping.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: fan-out to two additional targets (Telegram + Signal); one target failing does not block the other; `bestEffort` is correctly inherited.
- Edge cases checked: empty `additionalTargets` array is treated as no-op; targets with blank `accountId` are normalized correctly; primary delivery failure prevents fan-out (fan-out runs only after primary succeeds).
- What you did **not** verify: webhook-mode additional targets; more than three simultaneous additional targets under real network load.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `additionalTargets` is optional; existing cron jobs without it are unaffected.
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Remove `additionalTargets` from the affected cron job's delivery config via `openclaw cron edit <jobId>`.
- Files/config to restore: `src/cron/types.ts`, `src/cron/normalize.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `[additional-delivery]` warn lines appearing in gateway logs for targets that were expected to succeed; primary delivery being blocked (should not happen — fan-out is always fire-and-continue).

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A misconfigured `additionalTargets` entry silently fails without surfacing in the job's `lastRunStatus`.
  - Mitigation: Failures are logged at `WARN` level with the `[additional-delivery]` prefix and the target channel/address. A follow-up could surface per-target delivery status in `openclaw cron list` output.
- Risk: High-frequency cron jobs with many additional targets could increase outbound message volume significantly.
  - Mitigation: This is intentional behavior; operators control the target list. Rate-limit and allowlist policies already apply via `deliverOutboundPayloads`.

Closes #48026 
Closes #54935

target.title: Honor OpenAI thinking profiles over stale catalog opt-outs
target.body:
## Summary

- Lets OpenAI/OpenAI-Codex provider-owned thinking profiles override stale catalog `reasoning: false` rows.
- Keeps non-OpenAI `reasoning: false` catalog rows as explicit off-only opt-outs.
- Adds resolver and `/think` directive regressions for `openai/gpt-5.5` and `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

Closes #84646.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
`/think xhigh` on canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` could be rejected with `Use one of: off` when stale runtime catalog metadata marked the model `reasoning: false`, even though the OpenAI provider profile advertises non-off thinking levels for that model.

Real environment tested:
Local macOS worktree with bundled Node.js `v24.14.0` and repository dependencies.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
